coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of recent studies on electrochemical hydrogen storage by graphene-based materials: Impact of modification on overall effectiveness
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kopac, Turkan
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogen possesses a lot of potential as a renewable source of energy due to its high energy density and positive impact on the environment. It is expected to play a substantial role in various long-term applications. Considerable research effort has been directed toward identifying suitable materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage (EHS), including graphene-based materials. Graphene's exceptional properties, such as high electrical and thermal conductivity, electrochemical activity, chemical stability, and structural flexibility, make it an ideal candidate for hydrogen storage. This paper, given the profound importance of graphene in EHS and the existing knowledge gap regarding recent developmental trends and research directions, identifies current worldwide research advancements in graphene-based EHS and the impact of the modification on overall effectiveness by conducting a comprehensive search of literature published within the past five years. It presents a comprehensive comparison of various graphene-based materials, including doped graphene structures, metal hydride-graphene composites, graphene quantum dots, and graphene-alloy composites. It also outlines the main opportunities and obstacles of graphene-based materials in EHS research according to the investigated studies and provides insights and recommendations for forthcoming research in this area.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recent investigations on graphene-based structures for electrochemical hydrogen storage
Nitrogen and/or sulfur doping on graphene

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Masjedi-Arani and colleagues [173] synthesized porous carbon spheres (CSs) adorned with NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS and N-GQDs (sand-rose-shaped) using a one-step free-surfactant hydrothermal method. Tioglycolic acid (TGA) was utilized as both the S source and capping agent in the formation of Ni-Co sulfide-based materials (NCSs). The synthesis parameters, including the type of metal salt (Ni/Co), TGA and NaOH amounts, and pH were significant in controlling the morphology, size, and composition of the sulfide-based shell. The resulting 3D hierarchical N-GQD/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres (1st shell: Ni-Co sulfide; 2nd shell: N-GQDs on the CS substrate) were examined according to their porosity, dimensions, and morphology. These 3D microspheres proved to be promising for EHS because of the excellent electroconductivity of the N-GQDs and CS carbon materials combined with the high capacity of sand-rose-shaped NCS structures. Carbon microspheres were generated using a rapid sonochemical method, and various linear amines were employed as conductive supports. Fig. 17 shows the design of sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres and the hydrogen storage profiles. The EHS capacity, enhanced by decorating the carbon microspheres with sand-rose-shaped NCS/CoS/Co9S8 nanostructures, reached 930 mAh/g. The addition of N-GQDs to the 2nd shell, covering the NCS/CS structures, further improved the EHS behavior of the electrode (1244 mAh/g). Notably, N-GQDs significantly boosted the discharge efficiency of the core-double-shell spheres by creating trap positions through both N dopants and edge conditions. These traps adsorb charge transporters, enhancing storage performance. The improvement in the EHS was attributed to the positive impact of the N-GQDs, which enhanced the conductivity and created trap sites for hydrogen sorption.

Opportunities and challenges in the utilization of hydrogen energy and hydrogen storage.

list-item.para: •Hydrogen is a renewable clean energy carrier that supplies energy three times more than conventional energy sources. This presents an opportunity to use hydrogen as a reliable and sustainable energy source.

•Hydrogen can be produced through electrolysis from renewable energy sources such as wind and solar power. This allows for integrating renewable energy into the existing energy infrastructure and provides a way to store and transport renewable energy.
•Hydrogen can help reduce greenhouse gas emissions, making it an attractive option for addressing climatic concerns.

•Hydrogen and fuel cells have the potential to decarbonize the global energy system significantly. They can be used as a clean and sustainable alternative to fossil fuels in various sectors, such as transportation, industry, and power generation.

•Hydrogen can be used as an energy carrier and stored for later use. This can help address the intermittent nature of renewable energy sources such as solar and wind power, by storing excess energy during periods of low demand and releasing it when needed.
•Hydrogen storage can play a crucial role in integrating renewable energy sources, such as solar and wind power, by storing excess energy for later use.
•Improving the efficiency and capacity of hydrogen storage systems can contribute to its widespread adoption as an alternative fuel.

•Fuel cell vehicles (FCVs) have the potential to replace internal combustion engine vehicles and reduce greenhouse gas emissions. FCVs use hydrogen as fuel and produce only water vapor as a byproduct, making them environmentally friendly.
•Hydrogen-fuel cells offer effective solutions for vehicles if hydrogen can be stored and carried safely at a high density.

•Nanomaterials have shown great potential for improving the performance of hydrogen storage materials. Further research and development in this area could lead to the discovery of new materials with enhanced properties.
•Hydrogen is highly flammable and requires careful handling and storage. Ensuring the safety of hydrogen production, storage, and transportation is crucial for its widespread use.

•Scaling up hydrogen production to meet the demand for various applications can be challenging. The current production capacity of hydrogen is limited, and scaling up production while ensuring sustainability and minimizing environmental impacts is a challenge.

•Hydrogen production, storage, and distribution can be expensive. Hydrogen produced from renewable sources is more expensive than fossil fuel-based hydrogen production. The cost of fuel cells and hydrogen infrastructure must also be reduced for widespread adoption.

•The widespread adoption of hydrogen and fuel cells requires the development of a robust infrastructure, including hydrogen production facilities, storage systems, and refueling stations. Building this infrastructure can be challenging and requires significant investment.
•The efficiency of hydrogen production and fuel cell systems must be improved. Energy losses occur in hydrogen production, storage, and conversion, reducing the system's overall efficiency.
•Storing hydrogen energy poses practical problems, and developing a simple and safe storage method is a significant challenge, especially for electric vehicles.
•Developing efficient methods for storing hydrogen safely and cost-effectively is crucial.
•Developing cost-effective hydrogen storage systems is a major challenge. Storage technologies like compressed gas and liquid hydrogen can be expensive and require specialized infrastructure.

•Scaling up hydrogen storage systems to meet the demands of large-scale energy storage and transportation applications is challenging. Developing scalable and efficient storage solutions is necessary to adopt hydrogen as an energy carrier widely.
•Solid-state hydrogen storage (SSHS) methods, such as electrochemical hydrogen storage, are being explored as safer alternatives to gaseous and liquid-state storage methods.

•The development of efficient and durable materials for hydrogen production, storage, and fuel cells is an ongoing challenge. Research is needed to improve the performance and longevity of materials used in these systems.

•The selection of suitable materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage is a challenge. The material's chemical constitution, crystalline behavior, porous structure, and catalytic behavior all play a role in determining its hydrogen storage capacity.
•The need for materials with high gravimetric and volumetric storage capacities is challenging.
•Stability and durability of hydrogen storage materials.
•Designing and developing materials with higher hydrogen storage capacity is challenging in the field.
•The search for new storage materials with high specific surface area, high porosity, and low density is essential.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Opportunities and challenges of graphene-based materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage research.

list-item.para: •The graphene foam exhibited a high capacity for hydrogen storage.
•Graphene foam's porous 3D structure provides a high surface area and facilitates electrolyte mass transfer and diffusion.
•The charging curve of the graphene foam showed pseudocapacitance characteristics, indicating both physisorption and chemisorption processes.
•The graphene foam demonstrated excellent stability with no activation period or fast capacity decay after repeated cycles.
•Graphene foam's capacity decreases at higher applied currents due to the diffusion limitations of hydrogen ions within the foam.
•The presence of oxygen functional groups on the graphene surface can block active sites for hydrogen adsorption and diminish the hydrogen uptake capacity.
•The exact mechanism of hydrogen storage in the graphene foam needs further investigation.
•The stable chemisorption of atomic hydrogen on single-layer graphene (SLG) has a theoretical maximum storage capacity of 7.7 wt %, making it a promising material for hydrogen storage.
•Hydrogen adatoms can be easily produced under mild conditions by the electroreduction of protons in solid/liquid systems.

•SLG can carry out H-chemisorption under mild conditions via a Pt-electrocatalyzed "spillover-surface diffusion-chemisorption" mechanism, as demonstrated by dynamic electrochemistry and isotopic Raman spectroscopy.

•The apparent surface diffusion coefficient, capacity, and stability of hydrogen adatoms on SLG at room temperature and atmospheric pressure are significant, making them suitable for applications involving stored hydrogen atoms on graphene.
•Overall, the electrochemical storage of atomic hydrogen on SLG presents opportunities for high-density hydrogen storage.
•Generating hydrogenated graphene from H2 requires extreme temperatures and pressures.
•The performance of hydrogen storage on graphene by physical adsorption is disputed due to the difficulties in obtaining reproducible adsorption capacity measurements.
•Fulfilling the goal of high-capacity hydrogen storage on graphene under solid/gas conditions would require unrealistic conditions for daily use mobile applications.
•Carbon materials, including graphene, are inert to the electro-reduction of protons, but the Pt-electrocatalyzed spillover-surface diffusion-chemisorption mechanism can overcome this problem.
•The stability of chemically adsorbed hydrogen atoms on SLG under ambient conditions must be further investigated.
•Challenges in terms of scalability and practical implementation.
•Electrochemical hydrogen storage on nitrogen-doped graphene offers a potential solution for storing and utilizing hydrogen as an alternative to carbon-based energy services.

•Storing hydrogen in carbon/graphene-based materials, such as nitrogen-doped graphene, can help reduce the weight density issues associated with traditional hydrogen storage methods, such as storing hydrogen gas in confined form or metal hydrides.
•Nitrogen-doped graphene's storage capability needs to be enhanced. It is currently comparatively small and requires improvement to be made more practical and efficient.

•The production and implementation of nitrogen-doped graphene for electrochemical hydrogen storage may face challenges related to cost and scalability. Scaling up the production of nitrogen-doped graphene and making it cost-effective for large-scale applications could be a challenge that needs to be addressed.
•Graphene and its derivatives can be used for hydrogen storage, providing a high surface area and active sites for hydrogen adsorption.
•Incorporation of metals and nitrogen into graphene structures to enhance hydrogen storage capacity.
•Nitrogen-doped graphene for improved hydrogen adsorption and storage performance.
•Increased hydrogen adsorption capacity due to nitrogen bonds on the graphene foam.
•Unique 3D foam-like structure providing high surface area and active sites for hydrogen adsorption.
•Electrochemical hydrogen storage potential.
•Kinetics of hydrogen adsorption and desorption, requiring fast storage and release rates.
•Reversibility of the reaction, optimization of doping and structure, and understanding the mechanism are challenges.

•Nitrogen-doped graphene can potentially increase the hydrogen storage capacity compared to pure graphene or other materials. This is due to the presence of nitrogen atoms, which can interact with hydrogen molecules and enhance their adsorption.
•Nitrogen-doped graphene can facilitate faster adsorption and desorption of hydrogen molecules, leading to improved hydrogen storage and release kinetics.
•Nitrogen-doped graphene can be synthesized with tailored pore size and surface area, optimizing the hydrogen storage capacity and improving the material's overall performance.

•Nitrogen doping can be combined with other dopants, such as sulfur or transition metals, to create synergistic effects that further enhance the hydrogen storage properties of graphene-based materials.

•Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene with controlled doping levels and uniform distribution of nitrogen atoms can be challenging. Scaling up the synthesis process to produce large quantities of nitrogen-doped graphene for practical applications is also challenging.

•Nitrogen-doped graphene materials may have stability and durability issues, especially under harsh operating conditions. Nitrogen doping may affect the structural integrity of graphene and lead to degradation over time.
•The cost and availability of raw materials required for synthesizing nitrogen-doped graphene, such as graphene oxide and nitrogen sources, can be a limiting factor for large-scale production.

•Integrating nitrogen-doped graphene materials into existing hydrogen storage systems and infrastructure may require modifications and adaptations to ensure compatibility and efficient utilization of the material's properties.

•Hydrogen storage technologies, including those based on nitrogen-doped graphene, must address safety concerns related to hydrogen leakage, flammability, and potential hazards associated with high-pressure storage systems.

•Strontium chromate-nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene (SrCrO4/NSG) exhibited the highest discharge capacity compared to other nanocomposites, indicating its potential for electrochemical hydrogen storage.
•The introduction of nitrogen and sulfur dopants into graphene improved its electrocatalytic activity, creating chemically active sites and suitable sites for nucleation.

•The strong interaction between doped graphene and nanocrystals prevented the aggregation of nanomaterials on the graphene surface, leading to a uniform distribution of nanoparticles on the heteroatom-doped graphene.
•The nanoparticles showed excellent cycle stability, indicating their potential for repeated use in electrochemical hydrogen storage.
•The sol-gel method used to synthesize strontium chromate nanocrystals is a relatively simple and scalable technique.
•There is a lack of information on the stability and durability of the SrCrO4/NSG nanocomposite over long-term use, which is important for practical applications.
•The cost-effectiveness or scalability of the synthesis method for SrCrO4/NSG could be a limitation for large-scale production.
•Information on the specific conditions and parameters used in the electrochemical measurements, making it difficult to assess the reproducibility and reliability of the results.
•Hydrogen storage alloys have the potential to significantly increase the energy storage capacity of rechargeable power batteries in electric vehicles.
•Various studies have reported the development of hydrogen storage alloys with high electrochemical hydrogen storage ability.
•Nanostructured metal hydrides, which are a type of hydrogen storage alloy, have shown size-dependent hydrogen storage properties, indicating the potential for further optimization and improvement.
•Graphene-based nanomaterials, such as graphene/porous cobalt nanocomposites, have demonstrated noticeable electrochemical hydrogen storage ability at room temperature.
•Mesoporous carbon materials have been investigated for their potential in electrochemical energy storage and conversion, including hydrogen storage.
•Pillared graphene, a 3D network nanostructure, has shown promise for enhanced hydrogen storage.
•The electrochemical stability of hydrogen storage alloys, particularly in acidic electrolytes, is an important challenge that needs to be addressed.
•Determining the pore size of modified micro- and mesoporous materials, including those used for hydrogen storage, can be challenging and may have limitations.
•Further research is needed to optimize and improve the hydrogen storage capacity of alloys.
•The role of interlayer spacing and functional groups on the hydrogen storage properties of graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide is an ongoing challenge that requires further investigation.
•CdSnO3 nanostructures have potential applications in hydrogen storage systems due to their high hydrogen sorption capacity.
•The use of ultrasound irradiation in the synthesis process can prevent particle agglomeration and result in the formation of uniform and small-sized CdSnO3 nanoparticles.
•Adding graphene to CdSnO3 nanocomposites can enhance the hydrogen sorption capacity and improve the electrochemical efficiency of the fabricated electrode.

•The synthesis of CdSnO3 nanostructures requires optimization of various parameters such as ultrasound waves, calcination temperature, and type of solvent. Finding the optimal conditions can be time-consuming and challenging.
•To ensure their long-term performance, the stability and durability of CdSnO3 nanostructures in hydrogen storage systems need to be further investigated.
•The porosity of CdSnO3 nanostructures may limit their hydrogen storage capacity. Further research is needed to improve their porosity and enhance their hydrogen sorption properties.

•The synthesis of nanocomposites with good surface area and porosity, such as nickel zirconia reduced graphene oxide (Ni-ZrO2-rGO) and cobalt zirconia reduced graphene oxide (Co-ZrO2-rGO), can provide effective materials-for-sustainable-hydrogen storage.
•While nickel and cobalt nanoparticles are cheaper than metals like palladium and platinum, cost-effectiveness is still important in developing hydrogen storage materials.

•The capacity and kinetics of solid-state hydrogen storage methods, including electrochemical storage, depend on the interaction between the hydrogen and the specific material. Further research is needed to optimize nanocomposites' storage capacity and kinetics, such as Ni-ZrO2-rGO and Co-ZrO2-rGO.

•The electrocatalytic properties of the metal component, the impact of zirconia on the crystalline structure, and the porosity variation due to graphene properties all influence the electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites. Understanding and optimizing these factors is challenging in developing efficient electrochemical hydrogen storage materials.
•Cobalt aluminate (CoAl2O4) has high thermal resistance and electrical, optical, and catalyst properties, making it suitable for various applications.
•The addition of TiO2, SiO2, and graphene to CoAl2O4 can improve the performance of electrochemical hydrogen storage.
•Graphene has superior physical characteristics and can enhance the ability of hydrogen absorption into the host materials.
•TiO2 nanoparticles have desirable photocatalytic and redox abilities, which can improve the reduction/oxidation of hydrogen atoms.
•SiO2 nanoparticles can significantly improve the hydrogen storage characteristics of the host materials.
•The Coulombic efficiency of CoAl2O4 nanocomposites with TiO2, SiO2, and graphene is lower compared to pure CoAl2O4.
•The discharge capacity of CoAl2O4 nanocomposites may not increase significantly after a certain number of charge and discharge cycles.
•The aggregation of nanoparticles in CoAl2O4/TiO2 and CoAl2O4/SiO2 nanocomposites may affect the overall performance.
•The physical adsorption mechanism of hydrogen storage in the prepared samples may require high pressure and temperature for hydrogen desorption.
•The specific surface areas and pore sizes of CoAl2O4 and CoAl2O4/TiO2 samples may limit their hydrogen storage capacity.
•Graphene oxide (GO) has excellent potential in energy-saving applications, especially in hydrogen storage materials.
•GO coatings have shown a significant improvement in the amount of adsorbed hydrogen, with a hydrogen storage capacity increased from 88 to 741 F/g.
•Combining GO with metallic layers like nickel can improve corrosion resistance and hydrogen storage capacity.
•The use of multi-layer coatings, such as the three-layer GO/Ni/GO coating, can improve the performance of hydrogen adsorption and storage.
•The stability and efficiency of hydrogen storage materials, such as graphene oxide, must be further studied and improved.
•Optimizing deposition methods, such as electrophoretic deposition (EPD), is challenging to achieve the desired coating properties.
•Understanding and utilizing the hydrogen spillover phenomenon to improve hydrogen storage capacity is still a topic of research and development.

•The use of TiNb2O7 and GQDs as active materials in the nanocomposites has led to an increase in hydrogen storage capacity. The hydrogen storage capacity for S-GQDs/TN3 and N-GQDs/TN3 was calculated to be 1234 and 2433 mAh/g respectively after 15 cycles in the 6 M KOH electrolyte, compared to 979 mAh/g for pristine TiNb2O7 nanoparticles. This indicates that the nanocomposites have the potential to improve the efficiency of hydrogen sorption applications significantly.

•The nanocomposites exhibit excellent electrochemical properties, including excellent conductivity and rapid electron transfer. This is attributed to the structural characteristics of TiNb2O7 nanoparticles, which have active redox locations and facilitate electron transfer. The use of GQDs further enhances the conductivity and electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites.
•The solvothermal synthesis method allows for the control of the morphology of the nanocomposites. This adapted morphology contributes to their overall performance and functionality.

•The interconnected construction of graphene quantum dots in the nanocomposites is crucial in improving conductivity and electrocatalytic effects. This interconnected structure also enhances stability and facilitates hydrogen evolution reaction without the need for additional conducting materials.

•Achieving the desired hydrogen storage capacity requires optimization of the morphology of the nanocomposites and the concentration of the electrolyte. This optimization process may pose challenges in terms of finding the optimal conditions for maximum performance.

•The redox process involving the transition metal species in TiNb2O7 nanoparticles and hydrogen adsorption requires careful control and balance of charges. Understanding and optimizing this process may present challenges in synthesizing and applying the nanocomposites.

•The solvothermal synthesis method used to fabricate the nanocomposites may have scalability and industrial production limitations. Developing scalable synthesis methods while maintaining the desired properties and performance of the nanocomposites may be challenging.

•The cost-effectiveness and commercial viability of the nanocomposites need to be considered. The synthesis of the nanocomposites and their integration into practical applications may require further research and development to address cost and scalability challenges.
•Porous carbon spheres derived from resorcinol-formaldehyde (R-F) gels have various applications due to their favorable porosity and electro-conductivity.
•The method used in this work for forming carbon microspheres is fast and can be carried out at room temperature.
•Different basic catalysts, such as en, TETA, and TEPA, were utilized to control the size of the carbon spheres.
•Ultrasonic irradiation with the production of active species (OH and H) helps form hydroxymethyl-substituted samples and fast condensation and polymerization of R-F resin.
•The synthesized carbon spheres with different alkaline catalysts are microporous materials with an average size of less than 2 nm, making them favorable candidates for electrochemical applications.
•The N-GQDs obtained have three nitrogen types (pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphite N) and can be used for various applications.

•The NCS nanostructures synthesized using different precursor salts and pH conditions have different morphologies and compositions, providing opportunities for tailoring their properties for specific applications.
•The formation of carbon microspheres using different basic catalysts and ultrasonic irradiation requires optimization to achieve the desired size, regularity, and porosity.
•Characterizing CSs, N-GQDs, and NCS nanostructures requires various techniques such as FT-IR, XRD, FE-SEM, TEM, EDS, and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isothermal measurement.
•Synthesis of NCS nanostructures using different precursor salts and pH conditions requires careful control to obtain the desired morphology and composition.
•Characterizing and analyzing the obtained materials require expertise and specialized equipment.
•The application of CSs, N-GQDs, and NCS nanostructures in specific fields, such as electrochemical applications, may require further research and optimization to enhance their performance.

•The BaMoO4/ZnO-GQDs and BaMoO4/ZnO-GO nanocomposites showed higher electrochemical -performance compared to BaMoO4/ZnO, indicating improved hydrogen storage capacity. This suggests that these nanocomposites have the potential to be used as efficient materials for hydrogen storage applications.

•The use of green sources and biomass materials, in the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) is a sustainable approach. This aligns with the idea of a sustainable development strategy and promotes using renewable and waste materials.
•The BaMoO4/ZnO-GO nanocomposites exhibited a larger specific surface area than BaMoO4/ZnO. This provides more active sites for electrochemical hydrogen storage, making the process more efficient.

•The challenge lies in maintaining the structural properties of the graphene oxide (GO) and GQDs during the manufacturing process. It is important to ensure that the sheet-like graphite oxide structure of GO and the morphology of GQDs are not deformed, as they contribute to the electrochemical performance of the nanocomposites.

•The cost and availability of biomass materials may vary depending on the region. When considering the large-scale production of these nanocomposites, the cost-effectiveness and scalability of the green synthesis approach must be considered.

•Synthesis conditions, such as temperature and duration, are crucial in forming GQDs and CDs. Optimizing these conditions is important to achieving the desired properties and performance of the nanocomposites.

•The stability and durability of the BaMoO4/ZnO-GQDs and BaMoO4/ZnO-GO nanocomposites need to be further investigated. Long-term stability and resistance to degradation are important factors to consider for practical applications.

•Scaling up and commercializing these nanocomposites may pose challenges regarding production efficiency, quality control, and cost-effectiveness. Further research and development are needed to address these challenges and make these nanocomposites viable for industrial applications.

•The nanocomposite material showed a higher hydrogen storage capacity than pure HNTs. The hydrogen storage capacity of pure HNTs was 300 mAh/g, while that of S, N-GQD-coated HNTs was 466 mAh/g. The hydrogen storage capacity was further increased to 600 mAh/g by adding CoFe2O4 nanoparticles.

•The presence of CoFe2O4 in the nanocomposite facilitated the spillover procedure, in which hydrogen atoms migrate from the electrocatalyst nanoparticles to the surface of the HNTs. This spillover mechanism enhances the nanocomposite's hydrogen sorption ability.

•The S, N-GQDs act as conductive additives in the nanocomposite, allowing for quick charge transportation for hydrogen storage utilization. The presence of conductive additives improves the material's electrochemical performance.
•The nanocomposite material based on HNTs, S, N-GQDs, and CoFe2O4 shows promising opportunities for electrochemical hydrogen storage.

•The exact mechanism of the spillover process is still under debate. There is a lack of understanding of the exact mechanism and contradictory results on the hydrogen uptake characteristics. Further research is needed to fully understand and optimize the spillover mechanism in the nanocomposite material.

•The migration of hydrogen atoms from the electrocatalyst nanoparticles to the HNTs is a rate-limiting step in the spillover process. The diffusion of hydrogen atoms needs to overcome high energy barriers, which can affect the overall hydrogen storage performance of the nanocomposite.

•The nanocomposite material must be further optimized to improve its electrochemical performance and stability. Factors such as the nanocomposite's composition, morphology, and surface characteristics must be carefully controlled to achieve the desired hydrogen storage properties.

•Further research is needed to address the challenges associated with the spillover mechanism, rate-limiting steps, and material optimization to fully exploit this nanocomposite material's potential for energy storage applications.

•The Co9S8 + CoRGO composite showed a higher maximum discharge capacity (637.5 mAh/g) than other Co-S materials synthesized by different methods (e.g., a Co-S alloy synthesized by ball milling had a maximum discharge capacity of 480 mAh/g).

•The Co9S8 + CoRGO composite exhibited better cycle stability, with a higher value (77.9% for the 50th cycle) than other Co-S materials. This indicates that the composite can retain more of its discharge capacity after multiple charge/discharge cycles.

•The combination of Co and graphene in the CoRGO composite showed a synergistic effect, leading to improved electrochemical performance. The unique reticular globular morphology of CoRGO provided a 3D structure that improved electrocatalysis and charge transfer, while also relieving stress and maintaining the stability of the Co-based alloy. The large specific surface area of CoRGO provided more charge transfer channels and active sites, facilitating hydrogen transfer within the alloy.

•The loading of RGO and CoRGO on the Co9S8 alloy surface improved its corrosion resistance in alkaline electrolytes. The RGO and CoRGO coatings acted as protective layers, inhibiting the dissolution and oxidation of active metal species and enhancing the electrode's cycle stability.

•The optimal content of CoRGO in the Co9S8 alloy was found to be 6 wt%. Higher amounts of CoRGO (e.g., 9% and 12%) led to the detachment of CoRGO from the alloy surface, affecting the electrochemical reactions and reducing the electrochemical activities and capacities of the electrodes. Therefore, finding the right balance of CoRGO content is crucial for achieving optimal performance.

•The distribution of CoRGO within the Co9S8 alloy is important for its performance. The collision between CoRGO, Co9S8 powders, and steel balls during ball milling facilitated the anchoring and dispersion of CoRGO on the alloy surface. However, if the mass of the dopant is too high, a portion of CoRGO may gather and detach from the alloy surface. Therefore, ensuring a uniform and stable distribution of CoRGO is a challenge.
•Understanding the underlying electrochemical hydrogen storage mechanisms of the Co9S8 + CoRGO composite and optimizing the alloy composition and structure could further improve its performance.

•The addition of graphene to the LaY2Ni9.5Mn0.5Al0.5 alloy through ball milling treatment significantly improves its electrochemical hydrogen storage capacity. The LaY2Ni9.5Mn0.5Al0.5 milled alloy with 3 wt% graphene exhibits a superior maximum discharge capacity of 277.4 mAh/g with a capacity retention of 53.9% after 100 cycles, which is higher than that of the raw alloy.
•Graphene addition improves the high-rate dischargeability of the alloy electrode. The alloy electrodes with graphene additive show improved high-rate dischargeability compared to the raw alloy.

•The addition of graphene improves the cycling stability of the alloy electrodes. The capacity retention rate at the 100th cycle of the LaY2Ni9.5Mn0.5Al0.5 alloy with graphene additives is higher than that of the raw alloy.

•The optimal amount of graphene addition is crucial for achieving the best electrochemical performance. The maximum discharge capacity of the alloy electrodes increases with the range of graphene addition from 1 to 3 wt% but decreases when the addition amount exceeds 5 wt%.

•Excessive graphene addition can lead to the peeling off of graphene from the surface of the electrodes, resulting in more serious capacity degradation of the alloys. Finding the right balance between graphene addition and alloy stability is important to prevent corrosion and capacity degradation.

•The ball-milling treatment with graphene helps reduce the particle size and achieve a more uniform size distribution of the alloy particles. However, excessive graphene addition can lead to agglomeration and weak bonding of the alloy particles, which may affect the electrochemical performance of the electrodes.
•Improved high-rate dischargeability (HRD) of the Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy composites with graphene oxide (GO), nitrogen-doped graphene (NG), and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene (NSG).
•Enhanced the alloy composites' charge transfer and electrical conductivity with GO, NG, and NSG.
•Improved corrosion resistance of the alloy composites with GO, NG, and NSG, particularly NSG.
•Aggregation of graphene on the alloy surface, which can weaken the HRD performance.
•Diffusion of hydrogen can be hindered by excessive aggregation of graphene.
•Determining the optimal doping ratio of NSG for different applications.
•Achieving a homogeneous distribution of graphene and NSG on the alloy surface.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Major conclusions on graphene-based materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage research.

row.entry: Hydrogen is a promising renewable energy source with high energy density and environmental benefits.
Electrochemical hydrogen storage is an effective method of storing hydrogen.
Graphene is a promising material for electrochemical hydrogen storage applications due to its affordability, chemical inertness, lightweight nature, high surface area, and excellent conductivity.

Graphene-based materials have exceptional properties that make them ideal candidates for electrochemical hydrogen storage, including high electrical and thermal conductivity, electrochemical activity, chemical stability, and structural flexibility.
Mesoporous graphene structures, such as 3D porous graphene foam (GF), exhibit high EHS capacity and stability due to their interconnected pore network and dangling bonds.
Graphene modification has a significant impact on the effectiveness of electrochemical hydrogen storage.

Modifying graphene through functionalization, doping with heteroatoms (such as N, B, and S), and incorporation into nanocomposites enhances its electrochemical activity, conductivity, and EHS capacity.
Nitrogen and/or sulfur doping on graphene enhances its electrochemical capability and creates semiconducting properties, improving EHS performance.
Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) hold promise for EHS due to their porous structure, functional groups, and high surface area.
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit unique physicochemical properties and have potential applications in energy storage systems.

Metal-metal oxide nanoparticle-incorporated graphene-based materials show improved EHS capabilities, with the metal component affecting the electrocatalytic properties and the graphene component enhancing conductivity and providing active sites.

Graphene/alloy composites demonstrate enhanced EHS capacity and cycling stability compared to pristine alloys, with graphene contributing to conductivity and the alloy component facilitating reversible redox reactions.
The synthesis of high-quality graphene materials at an industrial scale and the development of scalable methods for generating GQDs remain challenges in applying graphene in EHS.

Further research is needed to optimize the synthesis methods, improve graphene-based structures' efficiency and cyclic stability, and explore novel materials that enhance electrode kinetics and mass transport for improved EHS performance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Future prospects on graphene-based materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage research.

row.entry: Further research can be conducted to optimize the synthesis processes of graphene-based materials to enhance their electrochemical hydrogen storage properties.
The performance of graphene-based materials can be further improved by exploring different doping techniques and incorporating other nanomaterials.
The development of scalable and cost-effective methods for the large-scale production of graphene-based materials can enable their practical applications in energy storage systems.
Investigating the long-term stability and cycling performance of graphene foam as an electrochemical hydrogen storage material is crucial for its practical implementation.
Integrating graphene-based materials with other energy storage technologies, such as batteries and supercapacitors, can develop hybrid energy storage systems with improved performance and efficiency.
Exploring novel graphene-based composites and nanocomposites can provide new insights into enhancing graphene materials' electrochemical hydrogen storage capabilities.
Understanding the fundamental mechanisms and kinetics of hydrogen storage in graphene-based materials can guide the design and optimization of future energy storage systems.
Investigating the environmental impact and sustainability of graphene-based materials for energy storage is important for their widespread adoption in the future.

The effect of different electrolytes and operating conditions on the electrochemical performance of graphene-based materials can be investigated to optimize their performance in specific applications.
The potential for functionalization of graphene foam to enhance its hydrogen storage capacity and stability can be explored.

Collaborative efforts between researchers, industry, and policymakers can be initiated to promote the commercialization and implementation of graphene-based materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage.

The application of advanced characterization techniques, such as in situ Raman spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, can provide valuable insights into the electrochemical behavior and surface chemistry of graphene-based materials for hydrogen storage.
Conducting more charge/discharge cycles to assess the long-term performance of graphene-based materials can further investigate their stability.

Conducting in-depth studies to understand the underlying mechanisms of hydrogen adsorption and desorption on the graphene foam surface could help optimize its performance and design. Investigating the scalability and cost-effectiveness of the graphene foam synthesis process would be crucial for its commercial viability and widespread adoption in hydrogen storage systems.

Collaborating with industry partners to develop prototype hydrogen storage devices using graphene-based materials as electrode materials could accelerate the translation of this research into practical applications.
Further research and development may lead to higher storage capacities than the 5.5 wt% aim set by the Department of Energy (DOE) for 2025, making hydrogen fuel cells more efficient for vehicles.

The electrochemical solid/liquid systems that generate and store atomic hydrogen can be further optimized. This includes exploring different catalysts, electrolyte solutions, and electrode configurations to improve the system's efficiency and performance.

Once the technology is further developed and optimized, efforts can be made to scale up the production and commercialize the electrochemical storage of atomic hydrogen on single-layer graphene. This would involve manufacturing processes, cost reduction, and integration into hydrogen fuel cell systems for various applications, such as vehicles and portable power devices.

NSG has shown a significantly higher discharge capacity than other graphene materials, suggesting that further optimization of the doping process and structural modifications could lead to even higher hydrogen storage capacities. Doping graphene with nitrogen and sulfur atoms increases its electrical conductivity, which can further improve the electrochemical performance of NSG as an electrode material. Future research could focus on optimizing the doping process to maximize the electrical conductivity of NSG.

The introduction of heteroatoms into the graphene lattice can affect its catalytic properties. Further research on NSG and other doped graphene materials could lead to a better understanding of their catalytic properties and the development of metal-free catalytic systems for various applications. NSG has shown competitive performance compared to metal-based electrode materials, making it a cost-effective alternative for electrochemical hydrogen storage. Future research could focus on scaling up the synthesis of NSG and optimizing its performance for practical applications.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of recent studies on electrochemical hydrogen storage by graphene-based materials: Impact of modification on overall effectiveness

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Zonguldak Bülent Ecevit University, 67100, Zonguldak, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Zonguldak Bulent Ecevit University, 67100, Zonguldak, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogen possesses a lot of potential as a renewable source of energy due to its high energy density and positive impact on the environment. It is expected to play a substantial role in various long-term applications. Considerable research effort has been directed toward identifying suitable materials for electrochemical hydrogen storage (EHS), including graphene-based materials. Graphene's exceptional properties, such as high electrical and thermal conductivity, electrochemical activity, chemical stability, and structural flexibility, make it an ideal candidate for hydrogen storage. This paper, given the profound importance of graphene in EHS and the existing knowledge gap regarding recent developmental trends and research directions, identifies current worldwide research advancements in graphene-based EHS and the impact of the modification on overall effectiveness by conducting a comprehensive search of literature published within the past five years. It presents a comprehensive comparison of various graphene-based materials, including doped graphene structures, metal hydride-graphene composites, graphene quantum dots, and graphene-alloy composites. It also outlines the main opportunities and obstacles of graphene-based materials in EHS research according to the investigated studies and provides insights and recommendations for forthcoming research in this area.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing energy demands in the context of a rapidly growing world population and the ongoing expansion of industrial processes in recent decades, the search for clean, low-cost energy-efficient renewable energy sources (RESs) has become a major issue [1]. Fossil fuels, the primary source of global energy demand, are nonrenewable and contribute to greenhouse gas emissions, atmospheric pollution, global warming, and climatic changes. Hence, the global energy demand has led to the search for renewable and pollution-free alternative energy sources as an inevitable solution [1–6]. Various non-renewable and RESs are depicted in Fig. 1 [5].

Hydrogen, an environmentally friendly, green, and versatile RES with a high energy density, is gaining attention among RESs such as wind, solar, and ocean energy. As one of the most abundant elements in the universe, hydrogen can be easily generated from RESs, with water serving as its only byproduct. It is considered one of the most likely alternatives to carbon-based energy systems [7–13]. Thus, daily studies focusing on hydrogen production and storage are increasing [1,14–24]. Fig. 2 shows the hydrogen production methods and utilization for various applications [25]. Hydrogen is anticipated to have extensive usage across a variety of applications, and it may eventually replace fossil fuels as a source of energy in the long run. Fig. 3 shows the global developmental trend of the hydrogen economy for the years 1850–2150 [25].

Hydrogen production, storage, recovery, and transportation are the major stages that should be considered for the optimal utilization of hydrogen as an energy resource [1,13,26,27]. Developing a secure and user-friendly storage method is a major obstacle to the adoption of hydrogen as a fuel in electric vehicles and mobile applications [8,28–30].

Currently, three primary methods exist for storing hydrogen: compressed gaseous, liquid cryogenic, and solid-state hydrogen storage. However, both liquefied and gaseous hydrogen storage options have drawbacks, including low storage density, the need for high-pressure tanks, costly equipment and materials, and safety concerns. Storing hydrogen in a liquefied state necessitates lowering its temperature to −252.7 °C and placing it within a well-insulated container under high vacuum conditions. This process requires storage tanks with excellent thermal insulation capabilities, leading to increased energy consumption. In contrast, the solid-state approach eliminates the requirement for costly and risky transportation due to the in situ generation, storage, and retrieval processes. Furthermore, the growing demand for electric vehicles and lightweight and portable electronic devices underscores the rising prominence of the solid-state method [1,2,4,8,13,17,31–41].

Hydrogen storage in the solid state is secure and boasts greater efficiency in hydrogen utilization than storing hydrogen in gaseous or liquefied form [17,42]. A diverse range of materials is employed for solid-state hydrogen storage, encompassing zeolites, polymers, carbonaceous materials, metal hydrides (MHs), complex MHs, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [4,43–46]. Hydrogen can be adsorbed through physical adsorption, chemical adsorption, or electrochemical means (electrosorption) within storage materials [47,48].

Physical adsorption involves hydrogen adsorption to the surface of a material. Activated carbon, carbon nanofibers (CNFs), carbon nanotubes (CNTs), covalent organic frameworks (COFs), and MOFs all feature microporous structures and can capture and store hydrogen through this process [49–51]. On the other hand, chemical hydride storage entails the breakdown of hydrogen molecules into atoms at specific temperature and pressure conditions. Subsequently, these hydrogen atoms are integrated into the lattice structure of the material. Chemical hydrides, comprising both MHs and complex MHs, demonstrate substantial storage capacity for hydrogen and offer promising applications across various fields [13,41,52–54].

Table 1 presents the opportunities and challenges in the utilization of hydrogen energy and hydrogen storage. Solid-state hydrogen storage through electrochemical means has become a notable and stable method and has recently drawn increased attention. In an alkaline setting, hydrogen atoms are absorbed at interfaces, facilitated by surface vacancies integrated into networks of defects. Additionally, hydrogen diffuses through the electrode materials during electrochemical decomposition in an aqueous electrolyte. This process eliminates the need for high-pressure conditions, enabling the adsorption of hydrogen to occur directly within the host material under ambient conditions [48,55–58].

Various materials to improve electrode design have been developed for EHS, with carbon-based materials standing out as a crucial category. These materials, renowned for their cost-effectiveness, exhibit favorable traits such as excellent electronic conductivity, chemical stability, low density, elevated heat resistance, and a high strength-to-weight ratio. These attributes make carbonaceous materials suitable for application in EHS. Additionally, their versatile bonding capacity, ranging from sp1 to sp2 or sp3, further enhances their suitability for this purpose [18,44,47,59–61]. CNFs, CNTs, fullerene, and graphene represent various examples considering the variety of carbon materials applied in EHS thus far [1,48,55,57,62–65].

EHS, while effective at storing hydrogen ions (H+), currently has a relatively limited capacity and necessitates improvements in its storage capability [13,22,66–68]. Considerable research effort has been directed toward identifying appropriate materials for EHS. The primary focus of these studies was to develop novel hydrogen storage materials featuring electrochemical activity, an elevated surface area, and porous structures [8,13,69,70].

While conventional alloy groups such as LaNi5 and Mg2Ni have the capacity to store a significant quantity of hydrogen, they are unable to release hydrogen entirely unless subjected to relatively high temperatures [71]. Owing to their substantial chemical stability, electrical conductivity and high surface area-to-volume ratio (approximately 2600 m2/g), carbon-based materials have become a focal point for researchers exploring their application in hydrogen storage technologies [72]. Conversely, notable endeavors have been made in recent years to examine and store hydrogen in lighter elements, such as carbon/graphene-based materials, aiming to address concerns related to weight density [22,73,74]. Considerable focus has been directed toward graphene-based materials due to their enhanced electrical conductivity compared to graphitic carbon, as well as their large specific surface area exceeding 2600 m2/g. The chemical tolerance exhibited by these materials adds significant advantages for their potential application in energy storage technologies [8,75]. Due to the inherent structural defects in graphene, numerous available sites enable the absorption of hydrogen atoms. This has prompted extensive research aimed at improving the hydrogen absorption capabilities of carbon-based materials [58,72]. Graphene, a resilient and flexible membrane, can undergo modifications or functionalizations to facilitate future applications in energy storage/conversion and hydrogen storage [22,76].

While graphene holds significant promise for hydrogen absorption, several studies have worked to enhance this capability by introducing surface defects and porous structures, as well as incorporating various elements to accomplish surface functionalization of graphene. Numerous density functional theory (DFT) models have proposed that decoration of a graphene surface with vanadium (V) or lithium (Li) nanoparticles (NPs) can further optimize its performance [77–80]. Studies have demonstrated that introducing a secondary metal-based component to a carbon structure enhances the hydrogen storage capability of graphene. These investigations have explored various transitional metals, palladium (Pd), nickel (Ni), titanium oxide (TiO2), and cobalt (Co) composites for this purpose [48,57,63,64,81,82]. Additionally, molecular dynamics simulations were employed to explore the hydrogen absorption characteristics of graphene under different conditions [72,83,84]. The literature indicates that incorporating nitrogen (N) and heteroatoms through doping is a viable method for enhancing the characteristics of graphene [72,85].

The properties of electrode materials are important in assessing the efficiency of energy storage systems. It is widely acknowledged that the utilization of nanostructured electrode materials can augment the efficiency and cyclic stability of energy storage devices. By reducing the distance of ion diffusion and the charge transport path, electrode kinetics and mass transport can be improved. Furthermore, the collaborative action of multiple mechanisms and storage processes can enhance the ionizability of electrode materials [86].

Several review papers have been published in the literature so far on the synthesis strategies of graphene and its derived materials. These papers mainly focus on various applications of graphene and its modifications such as biomedical and biotechnological applications, gas sensing applications, sustainable energy applications, energy storage and conversion applications, and electrochemical energy storage applications. However, there is a gap in the analysis of the recent progress in the utilization of graphene-based materials in EHS. This review aims to fill this gap by specifically analyzing the advancements made in the last five years in EHS utilizing graphene-based materials and exploring the impact of modifications on the overall effectiveness. The primary objective of this contribution is to offer a concise overview of the latest developments in the utilization of graphene and graphene-based composites investigated for EHS applications.

The article's layout is as follows: The Introduction, which is comprised of four subsections describing hydrogen energy, hydrogen storage, EHS, and the goal of this contribution, is presented in the first section. The second section covers the search strategy. Sections 3,4,5,6 present, respectively, the graphene and graphene structures, modification of graphene, metal hydride–graphene composites, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Section 7 is dedicated to the research findings of the analyzed studies on graphene-based structures for EHS in 5 subsections depicting a comprehensive comparison between the modification of graphene. In these subsections, studies conducted with graphene structures, nitrogen and/or sulfur doping on graphene, metal-metal oxide nanoparticle-incorporated graphene-based materials, nitrogen- and/or sulfur-doped GQD nanocomposites, and graphene/alloy composites were discussed. Section 8 summarizes the opportunities and challenges, Section 9 major conclusions, and finally, in light of the review work carried out, Section 10 offers insights into potential future trends and developments.

The insights and recommendations derived from this study can serve as valuable references and guidance for comprehending the recent applications and future prospects of EHS and graphene-based materials within the field of electrochemistry.

Given the profound importance of EHS and graphene-based materials within the realm of electrochemistry and the existing knowledge gap regarding their recent developmental trends and research directions, this study conducted a comprehensive search of literature indexed by the Web of Science Core Collection throughout the previous five years. This paper aims to provide an overview of the current worldwide research advancements in the applications of graphene in EHS published between 2019 and 2023.

The data collection process for the research literature relies on the Web of Science Index, specifically the Science Citation Index Expanded database from Clarivate Analytics, USA. Recognized as one of the premier databases globally, this source can enable researchers to comprehend the current development status and trends in the fields of EHS and graphene. The subject term used was “Electrochemical Hydrogen Storage+Graphene”. The types of documents considered included articles. Proceeding Paper and Book Chapters were not included in the search. Review articles were subsequently excluded. Languages in English were selected. An initial search was conducted on August 03, 2023. The database was updated on December 27, 2023. A total of 956 papers on EHS and graphene published during the years 2019–2023 were obtained. Furthermore, the titles and abstracts were examined, and papers that did not involve “Electrochemical Hydrogen Storage+Graphene” were eliminated. A total of 42 studies were returned from 2019 to 2023; among those 18 of them were eventually selected and included in the final evaluation.

Carbon materials, which are abundant and possess remarkable properties, hold immense potential across various applications. Among the various carbon allotropes, graphene, discovered in 2004, is the most promising [87]. Bianco et al. [88] proposed a recommended terminology for the graphene family. According to their suggestion, graphene was defined as a monolayer of hexagonally arranged, sp2-bonded carbon atoms that is not an intrinsic component of a carbon material but is freely suspended or attached to an external substrate. Fig. 4 shows the atomic structure of a carbon atom, the sp2 hybrids formation, and the crystal lattice of graphene [89]. The traditional method for obtaining graphene is through a top-down approach from graphite, which involves stacking graphene layers parallel to each other in a 3D crystalline structure with long-range order [90]. Fig. 5 illustrates a graphical representation of the production of graphene using various techniques, including bottom-up and top-down approaches. Laser synthesis can be carried out under either set of principles, depending on the operating conditions employed [91].

In the past decade, there has been considerable research interest in two-dimensional (2D) independent sp2-hybridized carbon allotropy monolayer sheets. This heightened interest is attributed to the exceptional properties of these materials, which include outstanding electrical, optical, and mechanical characteristics; an ultrahigh surface area; improved electrical and thermal conductivity; heightened ionic mobility; excellent chemical stability; superior mechanical strength; and structural flexibility [1,18,61,87,92–95]. Hence, the combination of these features positions graphene as an ideal candidate for diverse applications, and extensive research has been underway globally in recent years [87,91,96–98].

With its substantial surface area and notable pore volume, graphene has emerged as a potentially excellent material for EHS and gas adsorption [1,18,57,99]. Graphene, with its distinctive properties, can also serve as a suitable substrate for diverse nanostructures [100,101]. The use of graphene in energy storage materials holds great promise [11,102–104]. It is an affordable, chemically inert, and lightweight material. The primary benefit of employing graphene in energy storage lies in its utilization in electrical, electrochemical, and chemical devices [105,106]. Graphene, with its elevated electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic capabilities, has the potential to expedite electrochemical reactions [13,107,108]. Its substantial specific surface area further allows for a greater number of accessible active sites for the electrochemical reaction [101]. It is considered as a promising material for use as a cathode in energy storage batteries and SCs. Due to its porous structure and impressive electrical conductivity, graphene is a suitable choice for hydrogen storage electrodes. Additionally, the excellent flexibility of graphene helps alleviate the impact of volume changes [13,95,106,109–112].

Single-layer graphene (SLG) and multilayer graphene (MLG) are among the most prevalent graphene structures which are typically obtained through the exfoliation of graphite. However, the characteristics of SLG, MLG, and bilayer graphene (BLG) differ based on the number of well-defined stacked graphene layers with extended lateral dimensions. Altering the layer number can modify the electronic properties and influence the mechanical and dielectric properties. Theoretical calculations further reveal that the work function changes depending on the layers owing to the shift in the Fermi energy with respect to the Dirac point [87,113]. Fig. 6 shows the different methods of graphite oxide synthesis from graphite using oxidizing acids and intercalation of alkali metals [91]. Fig. 7 illustrates the layered structures of graphenes and bulk graphite [89,114].

Nevertheless, graphene undergoes stacking, which compromises some of its distinctive properties. For instance, the experimentally accessible surface area of graphenes falls significantly below the previously mentioned figure, due to the strong tendency of graphene layers to aggregate [61,115]. To address this challenge, a 3D porous graphene structure, known as graphene foam (GF), was introduced [115]. GFs feature a porous structure with an interconnected pore network, ensuring appropriate pathways and channels for rapid electron transfer and providing exceptional electrical conductivity. Noteworthy attributes of GF include low density and weight, a high strength-to-weight ratio, and a specific surface area [1,61,116–118].

Mesoporous graphene (MG), recognized as a prospective carbon-based material with diverse applications, has undergone extensive research due to its distinct characteristics, including remarkable chemical tolerance, favorable electrical conductivity, and exceptional mechanical properties [2,105,119,120]. Specifically, the soft template method for producing MG, employing a polymer template for easy and complete removal, is acknowledged for its ability to avoid the aggregation of graphene nanosheets, and the restoration of their intrinsic surface area [119]. When utilized for EHS, MG has a notable specific surface area that incorporates both mesopores and micropores. Micropores play a crucial role in hydrogen adsorption, while mesopores facilitate improved access to electroactive species [93,121–125]. The distinctive 3D interconnected pore structures of MG offer suitable pore sizes for immobilizing metal NPs and accommodate the volume fluctuations of these NPs throughout the charge-discharge cycle [8,81,93,125].

Graphene serves as a durable and adaptable membrane that can be modified or functionalized to enhance its applications in energy storage and conversion as well as hydrogen storage [22,126]. Graphene oxide (GO) holds promising potential in the fields of energy, environmental science, and biotechnology owing to the oxygen-involving functional groups in the structure [127–129]. The substantial surface area or porous structure of GO, along with its functional groups, plays a role in both the physisorption of hydrogen molecules and the dehydrogenation of hydrides. The structure and properties of GO are influenced by the specific synthesis strategy employed and the oxidation degree [130–133]. Besides the epoxide groups responsible for binding oxygen atoms, other identified functional groups include phenols, hydroxyl (OH-), carbonyl (C=O), and sulfur (S) impurities. S impurities are generated during GO synthesis using the Hummers' method, primarily owing to the inclusion of sulfuric acid during the process [130–135]. Various methods such as, chemical, photo-chemical, thermal, photo-thermal, microwave, bacterial, or microbial processes can be applied to GO to decrease its oxygen content and result in the formation of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [90]. Fig. 8 shows the important GO/graphite oxide reduction strategies into reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [91]. Fig. 9 shows the chemical reduction of GO, which is the most widely applied technique used for preparing chemically converted rGO [136]. Fig. 10 shows structures of graphene, GO, and rGO [90].

New research has indicated that doping graphene with a heteroatom, such as N, can modify its physical, optical, electrical, and structural properties [110,137,138]. There is a rising expectation that numerous catalytically active sites can be acquired. Additionally, the chemical doping of graphene results in the formation of a band gap, causing it to exhibit n-type semiconductor characteristics. The interactions between the lone-pair electrons of N and the π-system of graphene lead to a substantial increase in the electrocatalytic activity of graphene [139,140]. Graphene/GO-based materials that have been modified with metal oxides reportedly demonstrate improved performance in electrochemical energy storage applications [141]. Youssry et al. [142] pointed out the importance of electrode materials, such as reduced rGO and Ni(OH)2, in improving the performance of supercapacitors.

Graphene nanocomposites have garnered significant interest as electrode materials for EHS, primarily owing to their enhanced electrical conductivity compared to graphene and their ability to enhance the surface area up to 2600 m2/g [8,101]. In light of the intrinsic structural defects in the graphene framework, numerous sites exist for the absorption of hydrogen atoms [58,72]. In a study, Kumar et al. [143] discussed synthesizing and characterizing a nanohole-structured and palladium-embedded 3D porous graphene (3D Pd-E-PG) material for potential applications in hydrogen storage and CO oxidation. Agglomerated palladium NPs created the nanoholes in the graphene surface, and smaller palladium NPs were embedded inside the graphene layers. The nanoholes in the graphene surface provided reactive sites for hydrogen storage, and the embedded palladium NPs acted as catalysts for CO oxidation. Fig. 11 shows the synthesis route of the 3D Pd-E-PG structure and the corresponding SEM images [143]. A schematic diagram of the hydrogen uptake and spillover mechanism in the 3D Pd-E-PG nanoholes is shown in Fig. 12 [143]. It was reported that the 3D Pd-E-PG material had a higher hydrogen storage capacity and catalytic activity than palladium-decorated graphene (Pd-D-G) samples. Consequently, numerous investigations have been conducted to improve the hydrogen absorption capabilities of carbon materials.

The use of graphene materials introduces new possibilities in advancing hydrogen storage-based energy technologies. Both 2D structures involving various graphene-like materials (GLMs), such as Ni/GLM, and 3D structures, such as CNF/GLM, CNT/GLM, and Ni/CNF(CNT)/GLM, benefit from the application of relatively straightforward and easily scalable synthesis techniques, as highlighted by Tarasov et al. [144]. In their review, Tarasov et al. [144] explored advanced graphene-based functional composites combined with metals, alloys, intermetallic compounds, and their hydrides. These composites serve various purposes, including acting as hydrogen storage materials for safe and compact storage, electrode materials for Ni-MH batteries, highly efficient catalysts for the hydrogenation of organic compounds and metals, and integrating into hydrogen energy systems for hydrogen-based energy storage and backup electric power [144].

Incorporating such structures into the formulation of MH composites results in a substantial rise in the hydrogen sorption-desorption rate. This improvement is attributed to the elevated thermal conductivity of these composites. Additionally, such MH composites are readily compactible, making them suitable for use in generators and portable hydrogen storage units. Composites prepared with carbon nanostructures, MHs, or hydroxide materials exhibit favorable effectiveness in Ni-MH batteries. The specific electrical conductivity of the Ni hydroxide-carbon nanostructure composites (3 wt %) surpasses that of pure hydroxide by more than seven orders of magnitude and exceeds the conductivity of the composite, including graphite, at the same weight ratio by five orders of magnitude. Using MH-carbon composites can boost the specific capacity of electrodes by 10–20%, especially at high current densities. The usage of MH-graphene composites in hydrogen storage and compression systems has the potential to improve the performance of MH hydrogen storage tanks significantly. Additionally, MH-graphene composites can be utilized in hydrogen-based energy systems for backup and storage [144].

Over the past few decades, carbon materials featuring 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D structures (CQDs, GQDs, CNTs, graphene, graphite, graphite oxide) have been extensively employed across various research domains, particularly in the field of hydrogen storage, due to their highly beneficial properties [145–148]. Graphite oxide is a carbon-based, multilayered structure that results from the oxidation of graphite using acid. It boasts several unique features, such as a modifiable surface structure, a range of functional groups, changes in the spacing between graphite layers due to acid reactions, and a mix of carbon atoms with hybridization of sp2 and sp3 [149–151].

As the size decreases in all three dimensions, distinct structures are created featuring a platform composed of one or more graphene layers and a crystal structure resembling graphite, referred to as GQDs [152–154]. Recently, GQDs incorporating functional associations have emerged as novel functional nanomaterials. These materials indicate excellent chemical and physical characteristics due to their robust quantum confinement and edge effects [155–157].

GQDs are defined as tiny fragments of graphene with a 2D lateral size smaller than 100 nm. In most cases, GQDs are used interchangeably with graphene nanosheets, which typically possess less than 10 nm lateral dimensions, representing the lower limit for graphene nanosheets [156,158,159]. This distinctive graphene family member is renowned for its impressive fluorescence characteristics. In addition to fluorescence, GQDs also exhibit qualities such as low toxicity, effective surface grafting via a conjugated π-π network, unique spin properties, a high surface area and photostability. They can be formed through top-down approaches such as hydrothermal/solvothermal methods, oxidative cleavage, microwave processes, ultrasonic-assisted processes, electrochemical oxidation, chemical vapor deposition, pulsed laser ablation processes, and electron-beam irradiation, as well as bottom-up approaches, including the molecular carbonization of organic molecular polymers and controlled synthesis in organic solvents [87]. The GQDs synthesis approaches are shown in Fig. 13 [160–163]. The fundamental concept behind GQD synthesis involves the breakdown of carbon-based precursors through repeated oxidation and reduction processes. Nevertheless, developing a scalable synthesis method for generating high-quality GQDs remains a significant obstacle. GQDs exhibit a highly crystalline structure, and their band gap energy can be adjusted in the range of 0–6 V by the modification of their surface properties and 2D size. These recently generated fluorescent materials have found effective applications in bioimaging, sensors, drug delivery, solar cells, SCs, secondary batteries, etc. To further enhance their characteristics, including electrical and electrochemical properties and catalytic activities, these GQDs have been subjected to doping with heteroatoms such as N, boron (B), and S [87].

GQDs possess unique physicochemical properties as functional carbon sources and play essential roles in energy storage [157,164,165]. Although there are only limited studies on the utilization of GQDs in highly efficient energy storage systems, previous studies have highlighted the incorporation of GQDs in electrode structures for investigating electrochemical energy storage systems. GQDs, which are composed of sp2-bonded carbon atoms possessing a considerable surface area, can be linked to ternary transition metal sulfide-based materials by means of their functional groups. This integration imparts hybrid structures with unique structural properties and synergistic electrochemical characteristics from both components. Recently, GQDs that have been functionalized with significant physicochemical properties have been widely used, specifically in energy storage applications. The application of GQDs in energy storage platforms extends to various electrochemical energy storage devices, including photocatalysts, SCs; Li-ion, Li-S, and Na-ion batteries [155–157,166–173]. GQDs possessing oxygen functional groups, edge effects, and a large surface area are particularly attractive for use in electrochemical applications [174,175].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recent investigations on graphene-based structures for electrochemical hydrogen storage
ce:section.ce:para: Recent investigations on various graphene-based structures, such as N and/or S-doped graphene, metal-metal oxide NP-incorporated graphene-based structures, N- and/or S-doped GQD nanocomposites, and graphene/alloy composites for EHS have been presented in Table 2 . In the table, the type of EHS method applied, and the final EHS capacity obtained have been indicated for the references investigated.

Ghorbani et al. [1] utilized a hydrothermal approach to synthesize GF, a 3D graphene structure characterized by well-developed interconnected pores. The EHS properties of the GO and GF were assessed through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) analyses. The porous architecture of GF exhibited exceptional EHS performance, demonstrating high capacity (321 mAh/g) and stability. Even after 100 cycles, the electrode exhibited a minimal capacity loss of only 2.5%, indicating excellent stability. Owing to its porous architecture and dangling bonds, the 3D porous GF structure showed great potential for EHS applications.

He et al. [176] illustrated the electrodeposition of a monolayer of atomic hydrogen and the stability of chemically adsorbed hydrogen atoms on SLG. The observed apparent surface diffusion coefficient, hydrogen storage capacity (∼6.6 wt%, exceeding 85% surface coverage), and stability of hydrogen adatoms on SLG at atmospheric pressure and room temperature were noteworthy. The adsorption capacity under normal conditions surpassed the target set for 2025 by DOE of 5.5 wt%, demonstrating the excellent suitability of the material for EHS applications. The results indicated that the Pt-electrocatalyzed mechanism, which involves spillover, surface diffusion, and chemisorption on graphene, was promising for hydrogen storage, given that the chemisorbed graphene layers can release H2 upon moderate heating.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen and/or sulfur doping on graphene
ce:section.ce:para: Doping foreign atoms into graphene is a technique that induces a semiconducting gap and transforms it into an n-type semiconductor. Consequently, the electrochemical capability of the electrode becomes notably impressive. Graphene modified with affordable and readily accessible heteroatoms such as N, B, and S devoid of any metallic components has the potential to exhibit noteworthy electrocatalytic performance, rivaling that of their metal-based counterparts [109].

Gunasekaran et al. [22] investigated the EHS capabilities of pure graphene and N-functionalized graphene (NGR) by conducting structural, capacitive, and faradaic energy studies. The N-functionalized graphene demonstrated outstanding EHS performance. The discharge capacities for pure graphene and NGR were 52 and 38 mAh/g, with maximum specific capacitances of 273 and 374 F/g, respectively. The study indicated that NGR contributed for a 37% improvement in EHS.

Aghajani et al. [72] synthesized N-doped 3D nanostructured GFs via hydrothermal methods and assessed their hydrogen storage capability (6 M KOH solution) through EIS, GCD, and CV analyses. The N-doped GF (1916.5 mAh/g) exhibited a significant improvement in EHS as compared to pure graphene due to the higher electronegativity of N, which resulted in a higher amount of electroactive sites on the surfaces of the GFs.

In their study, Ramezani and Dehghani [110] explored the EHS capabilities of N-and S-co-doped graphene (NSG), which was synthesized through a hydrothermal process using urea and thiourea as N and S sources. The NSG exhibited superior electrical conductivity and electrochemical efficiency when compared to N-doped graphene (NG) and GO. The schematic diagram of the preparation of materials and working electrode is shown in Fig. 14 . Improving the electrical conductivity of graphene led to an increase in EHS capacity. The discharge capacity of NSG (2418 mAh/g, 8.55 wt% hydrogen) surpassed the discharge capacities of NG (1663 mAh/g, 5.88 wt% hydrogen) and GO (653 mAh/g). This notable improvement in performance is attributed to the heightened electrical conductivity achieved through heteroatom doping. The introduction of heteroatoms also affects the magnetic characteristics of graphene. Graphene doped with heteroatoms (NSG, NG) has emerged as a significant candidate electrode material for EHS because of its rival metal-containing counterparts. This advancement may pave the way for the creation of cost-effective and metal-free catalytic systems for different applications.

In another study, Ramezani and Dehghani [101] employed the sol-gel technique to create N- and S-co-doped graphene decorated with strontium chromate (SrCrO4) nanocrystals. The EHS abilities of SrCrO4 and its related nanocomposites (SrCrO4(800), SrCrO4(1000), SrCrO4/rGO, SrCrO4/NSG) were investigated using chronopotentiometry (CHP). The SrCrO4/NSG nanocrystals exhibited superior EHS capabilities. The findings suggested that utilizing doped graphene as an NP substrate could increase the electrocatalytic activity of SrCrO4(1000) nanocrystals. The NPs also demonstrated excellent cycling stability, with thermal treatment-induced oxygen vacancies in monazite-type structures improving redox reactions, thereby enhancing sample conductivity and EHS capacity.

Seo and colleagues [120] synthesized Co metal NP-incorporated graphene nanohybrids (Co-MGs), through a decomposition process involving the metal precursor dicobalt octacarbonyl (Co2(CO)8) in the presence of a 3D structure MG (860 m2/g) allowing for easy diffusion of electroactive species. The analysis results indicated that Co metal was effectively immobilized on MG with a cubic phase structure. Even after hybridization with Co NPs, the 3D pore structure of Co-MGs was maintained. The optimized sample (Co-MG-45) demonstrated a high EHS (250 mAh/g) and good cycling stability (80% retention after 20 cycles). This suggests that the synergistic effect between the Co metal and the host MG makes Co-MGs a promising EHS material.

Masjedi-Arani and colleagues [177] employed a sonochemical method utilizing glucose as a green capping agent to synthesize cadmium stannate (CdSnO3) nanostructures.
