The product size and structure optimization involved investigating the calcination temperature, ultrasonic waves, and solvent type. Ultrasound irradiation was shown to be effective at preventing particle agglomeration. The CdSnO3/graphene nanocomposite electrode exhibited better EHS performance than the pristine CdSnO3 nanostructures.

Kaur and Pal [99] synthesized metal, metal oxides, and carbon components containing nanocomposites. Ni-zirconia-reduced GO (Ni–ZrO2–rGO) and Co-zirconia-reduced GO (Co–ZrO2–rGO) were synthesized hydrothermally and applied to Ni foam. Electrochemical studies were performed (6 M KOH electrolyte) to assess their EHS capacity. Schematic diagram depicting the synthesis of materials and preparation of electrode is shown in Fig. 15 . Ni–ZrO2–rGO had a larger surface area (185.46 cm2/g) than Co–ZrO2–rGO (99.51 cm2/g). The discharge capacity of Ni–ZrO2–rGO was determined to be 416.12 mAh/g (1.54 wt%), while that of Co–ZrO2–rGO was 291.38 mAh/g (1.07 wt%). These nanocomposites, which incorporated metal, ceramic, and carbon components, exhibited a substantial influence on the hydrogen storage capacity (HSC). Each constituent played a significant role, with the metal component affecting the electrocatalytic properties. The Ni nanocomposite displayed a higher discharge capacity than the Co nanocomposite. The superior catalytic role of Ni in hydrogen storage was attributed to its conductivity and defects. Zirconia influenced the crystalline structure, and the properties of graphene led to variations in porosity [99].

Co aluminate (CoAl2O4), a blue pigment with a normal spinel structure, was synthesized by Yan et al. [106] through thermal decomposition utilizing olive leaf extract to assist in precipitation. Additionally, nanocomposites of CoAl2O4/TiO2, CoAl2O4/SiO2, and CoAl2O4/graphene were synthesized, and the hydrogen storage capacities of these nanostructures were examined. The incorporation of TiO2, SiO2, or graphene with CoAl2O4 resulted in a reduction in the Coulombic efficiency. The maximum discharge capacity and Coulombic efficiency were related to CoAl2O4/TiO2 and CoAl2O4, respectively. CoAl2O4 and CoAl2O4/TiO2 had a maximum Coulombic efficiency (62%) and discharge capacity (2800 mAh/g) after 15 cycles of charge/discharge (±1 mA), respectively.

Owing to its distinctive physical and chemical characteristics, GO holds great promise in hydrogen storage applications, both as a bulk material and as a coating material in layers. In a study by Shojaeinia et al. [133], a three-layer GO/Ni/GO coating was applied to the surface of Ni-foam using a hybrid coating procedure, and the EHS performance was assessed using CV, GCD, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) (1 M NaOH solution). The results revealed a substantial enhancement in the EHS performance. An increase in the hydrogen storage capacity from 88 to 741 F/g was observed, while the load transfer resistance was reduced from 260 to 35 Ω for the uncoated and coated porous substrates, respectively. This improvement was due to the high specific surface area achieved. The EHS capacity of the synthesized coatings revealed that the coating obtained at 60 V within 2 h exhibited the highest capacity (741 F/g), while extending the coating time from 1 to 2 h led to more GO coating on the Ni-foam surface, thereby enhancing the storage rate. However, with an increase in voltage beyond 60 V, GO covered the cavities in the Ni foam, reducing the specific surface area and decreasing the storage rate to 465 F/g (80 V, 2 h).

Given the low rate performance and EHS capacity of transition metal oxides, the development of electrode materials has become a crucial strategy for enhancing electrochemical performance, offering electroactive sites, and facilitating fast electron/ion diffusion routes [148]. Also, there is a need to enhance the efficiency and durability of mixed metal oxide-supported materials that actively participate in energy storage processes [157].

In their work, Ghiyasiyan-Arani and Salavati-Niasari [157] employed a solvothermal synthesis method to synthesize bifunctional nanocomposites based on titanium niobate (TiNb2O7) NPs (TNs) as a mixed metal oxide and S- or N-doped GQDs (S-GQDs, N-GQDs). Fig. 16 shows the schematic diagram for working electrode structures and hydrogen storage profiles. S/N-doped GQDs enhanced the electron richness of the nanocomposites, improving the conductivity, while the pyridinic N generated electrophilic active sites, facilitating EHS. The remarkable electrochemical capability of the GQD/TN nanocomposites was attributed to the synergistic effects of TN and GQDs, which provided valuable active sites, easy electrolyte accessibility, hydrogen diffusion, and also contributed to the excellent conductivity and efficient structure of the material. The EHS capacity of the unmodified TN microspheres was 200 mAh/g, while that of the TN NPs was 979 mAh/g. Modification with GQDs significantly enhanced the EHS capacity. Through optimization of the morphology and an increase in the electrolyte concentration (6 M KOH), the EHS capacity was further improved. The GQD system, characterized by electron-rich structures and interconnected construction, exhibited enhanced conductivity. Additionally, the presence of pyridinic-N-oxide created electrophilic positions, supporting H+ sorption on the NPs [157].

Masjedi-Arani and colleagues [173] synthesized porous carbon spheres (CSs) adorned with NiCo2S4/Co9S8/CoS and N-GQDs (sand-rose-shaped) using a one-step free-surfactant hydrothermal method. Tioglycolic acid (TGA) was utilized as both the S source and capping agent in the formation of Ni-Co sulfide-based materials (NCSs). The synthesis parameters, including the type of metal salt (Ni/Co), TGA and NaOH amounts, and pH were significant in controlling the morphology, size, and composition of the sulfide-based shell. The resulting 3D hierarchical N-GQD/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres (1st shell: Ni-Co sulfide; 2nd shell: N-GQDs on the CS substrate) were examined according to their porosity, dimensions, and morphology. These 3D microspheres proved to be promising for EHS because of the excellent electroconductivity of the N-GQDs and CS carbon materials combined with the high capacity of sand-rose-shaped NCS structures. Carbon microspheres were generated using a rapid sonochemical method, and various linear amines were employed as conductive supports. Fig. 17
shows the design of sand-rose shaped N-GQDs/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres and the hydrogen storage profiles. The EHS capacity, enhanced by decorating the carbon microspheres with sand-rose-shaped NCS/CoS/Co9S8 nanostructures, reached 930 mAh/g. The addition of N-GQDs to the 2nd shell, covering the NCS/CS structures, further improved the EHS behavior of the electrode (1244 mAh/g). Notably, N-GQDs significantly boosted the discharge efficiency of the core-double-shell spheres by creating trap positions through both N dopants and edge conditions. These traps adsorb charge transporters, enhancing storage performance. The improvement in the EHS was attributed to the positive impact of the N-GQDs, which enhanced the conductivity and created trap sites for hydrogen sorption.

In their study, Karkeh-Abadi et al. [148] explored the effects of GQDs and GO on the EHS capacity by synthesizing BaMoO4/ZnO-GQDs and BaMoO4/ZnO-GO composites. A sonochemical method was utilized to produce nanostructured BaMoO4/ZnO using tween 20 as a stabilizer. GQDs were synthesized using commercial GO and Spiraea crenata. The specific surface areas of the BaMoO4/ZnO-GQDs (11 m2/g) and BaMoO4/ZnO-GO (124 m2/g) nanocomposites increased compared to that of BaMoO4/ZnO (9.1 m2/g). These resulting nanocomposites were employed as electrode materials in EHS through CV and chronopotentiometry (CP) tests. The findings indicated hydrogen storage capacities of 129 (BaMoO4/ZnO), 213 (BaMoO4/ZnO-GO), and 284 mAh/g (BaMoO4/ZnO-GQDs) after 15 cycles (2 M KOH electrolyte), confirming the impact of GQDs and GO additives on the development of electrochemical behaviors of nanostructures. The excellent EHS performance was attributed to the redox pair reaction of Mo6+/Mo5+ at active sites during the EHS process, presenting promising sorption electrode materials for enhancing the intrinsic performance of conductive compounds.

S-GQDs and N-GQDs, which serve as conductive elements, and Co-ferrite (CoFe2O4) NPs, which function as electrocatalysts, were added to halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), which are naturally occurred layered mineral supports with abundant active sites. This design, as reported by Ghiyasiyan-Arani and Salavati-Niasari [86], resulted in the creation of an efficient and environmentally friendly active material. CoFe2O4/S and N-GQD/HNT nanocomposites were synthesized through a green hydrothermal technique to enhance hydrogen storage sites and facilitate rapid charge transport for efficient EHS applications. The EHS capacity of the pure HNTs was measured at 300 mAh/g (2 M KOH electrolyte, after 20 cycles). In comparison, when HNTs were coated with S-GQDs and N-GQDs, the hydrogen storage capacity increased to 466 mAh/g, as determined by the charge/discharge CP method under the same conditions. Additionally, the introduction of CoFe2O4 into halloysite nanoclays enhanced the hydrogen sorption capability through spillover phenomena. Fig. 18 shows the decoration process of S, N-GQDs and CoFe2O4 NPs on HNTs and the mechanism for EHS process. The EHS capacity of the CoFe2O4-coated HNTs was 450 mAh/g, whereas the designed nanocomposites featuring CoFe2O4/S and N-GQD-coated HNTs exhibited an even higher capacity (600 mAh/g). Halloysite nanoclay and modified halloysite have demonstrated promise as electrode materials for EHS in alkaline environments. Notably, the ternary nanocomposites comprising CoFe2O4/S and N-GQD/HNT exhibited enhanced hydrogen sorption behavior. This improvement was attributed to the combination of conductive additives, physisorption, and spillover mechanisms. The synthesis of CoFe2O4/S,N-GQS/HNT nanocomposites offered a straightforward approach for the systematic design of promising compounds for energy storage systems [86].

Liu, Liu, Chen et al. [11] utilized a one-pot reduction process to produce a Co/graphene composite (CoRGO) which displayed a distinctive reticular globular morphology. To prepare the Co9S8 alloy, mechanical alloying was employed, and different quantities of CoRGO were coated onto the alloy surface through ball milling. EHS measurements were carried out in a standard trielectrode system. The CoRGO-coated Co9S8 electrode exhibited a superior discharge capacity compared to that of the RGO-modified alloy and the original alloy. With a CoRGO additive content (6 wt%), the maximum discharge capacity reached 637.5 mAh/g, and the high-rate dischargeability and the cycle stability of the electrode were improved. The Co particles within the CoRGO electrode actively engaged in reversible redox reactions, while the graphene component contributed to the high conductivity. Fig. 19
shows the schematic diagram for the fabrication of Co9S8 + CoRGO and the discharge capacities. The CoRGO structure not only enhanced the electrocatalytic activity but also increased the specific surface area of the alloy. The presence of a CoRGO protective layer likely restricted the dissolution and oxidation of Co metal. The improvement in reaction kinetics was attributed to the unique reticular globular morphology and 3D structure of CoRGO, which enhanced the electrocatalytic activity of RGO and increased the specific surface area. The synergistic effect of Co and graphene in the CoRGO composite played a crucial role in facilitating hydrogen diffusion, expediting charge transfer within/on the alloy, enhancing corrosion resistance, and consequently enhancing the electrochemical performance and reaction kinetics of the Co9S8 alloy [11].

Li, Yin et al. [95] employed ball milling processing to synthesize composites of LaY2Ni9.5Mn0.5Al0.5 mixed with graphene and investigated the influence of graphene addition on the electrochemical characteristics of the alloy. The introduction of graphene and ball milling had a significant positive impact on the EHS performance of the alloy. Specifically, the milled alloy with 3 wt% graphene demonstrated a superior maximum discharge capacity (277.4 mAh/g) with a capacity retention of 53.9% after 100 cycles, surpassing that of the raw alloy. The exchange current density of the electrodes and the diffusion coefficient of hydrogen also improved notably with the addition of graphene. The dischargeability of the alloy electrode was efficiently enhanced by the presence of graphene.

Fan et al. [13] prepared a Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy by a mechanical alloying process. N-doped graphene (NG) and N–S co-doped graphene (NSG) were obtained through a hydrothermal technique. To enhance the EHS capacity, 5 wt% GO, NG, and NSG were incorporated into a Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy using ball milling. Fig. 20 depics the schematic diagram for the Co0.9Cu0.1Si + NSG composite synthesis process. The EHS performance of the material was assessed using a LAND and a three-electrode battery system. The composite exhibited superior discharge capacity and enhanced cycling stability compared to the pristine alloy. The alloy doped with 5% NSG demonstrated the highest performance, achieving a discharge capacity of 580.1 mA/g—significantly greater than that of the pristine alloy (524.3 mAh/g). The inclusion of GO, NG, and NSG led to an improvement in the discharge capacity of the alloy, due to the high specific surface area and conductivity of graphene. The electrocatalytic performance of graphene was enhanced by the co-doping of N and S, leveraging the excellent electrical conductivity of graphene. The incorporation of N and S into graphene contributed to improved electrocatalytic activity, and when N and S were added to the Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy, the electrochemical activity and reaction kinetics of the alloy increased. The uniform distribution of NSG on the alloy surface provided additional channels for hydrogen transmission, accelerated charge transfer, and enhanced hydrogen permeability. Doping NSG proved to be an efficient method for enhancing the EHS capacity of the Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy [13].

Graphene-based materials have plenty of advantages in the utilization of EHS. However, there are also many challenges that need to be further investigated. Table 3 outlines the main opportunities and limitations of graphene-based materials for EHS research according to the investigated studies. The production of uniform, monodisperse, homogeneous, high-quality materials at an industrial scale is still the major limitation in its practical application. Additionally, the zero bandgap influences the efficiency of optoelectronic devices [162]. Developing a scalable synthesis method for generating high-quality GQDs remains a significant obstacle facing the scientific community.

The significant decrease in the experimentally accessible surface area of graphene due to the stacking of graphene layers, which arises from the strong tendency of graphene layers to aggregate, is also an important limitation in applications. To address this challenge, a 3D porous graphene structure, GF, featuring a porous structure with an interconnected pore network, ensuring appropriate pathways and channels for rapid electron transfer and providing exceptional electrical conductivity is introduced. Noteworthy attributes of GF include low density and weight, a high specific surface area, and a high strength-to-weight ratio [1,61,115].

This paper, given the profound importance of graphene in EHS and the existing knowledge gap regarding recent developmental trends and research directions, identifies current worldwide research advancements in graphene-based EHS and the impact of the modification on overall effectiveness by conducting a comprehensive search of literature published within the past five years. A number of graphene-based materials, including doped graphene structures, metal hydride-graphene composites, graphene quantum dots, and graphene-alloy composites, have been evaluated for their effectiveness in hydrogen storage. The study provides insights and recommendations for future research in the field of graphene-based EHS.

These studies aim to develop novel EHS materials with high electrochemical activity, large surface areas, and porous structures, as the characteristics of electrode materials are crucial to the efficiency of energy storage systems, so selecting the appropriate materials can augment the efficiency and stability of EHS devices. By reducing the distance of ion diffusion and the charge transport path, electrode kinetics and mass transport can be enhanced.

Graphene is a promising material for EHS applications due to its affordability, chemical inertness, and lightweight nature. Additionally, its high surface area and pore volume provide a larger number of active sites for electrochemical reactions. The flexibility of graphene also helps to mitigate the effects of volume changes. It is an ideal choice for hydrogen storage electrodes.

Considerable focus has been directed toward graphene-based materials due to their enhanced properties, including outstanding electrical, optical, and mechanical characteristics; ultrahigh surface area; improved electrical and thermal conductivity; heightened ionic mobility; excellent chemical stability; superior mechanical strength; and structural flexibility. The combination of these features positions graphene as an ideal candidate for EHS applications.

Mesoporous graphene is a prospective material for EHS due to its remarkable chemical tolerance, electrical conductivity, mechanical properties, and notable specific surface area incorporating both mesopores and micropores. Micropores play a crucial role in hydrogen adsorption, while mesopores facilitate improved access to electroactive species. The 3D interconnected pore structures offer suitable pore sizes for immobilizing metal NPs and accommodate the volume fluctuations of the NPs throughout the charge-discharge cycle.

GO holds promising potential due to the oxygen-involving functional groups in its structure. The substantial surface area/porous structure, along with its functional groups, plays a significant role in EHS. The structure and properties of GO are influenced by the specific synthesis method employed and the oxidation degree.
Graphene can also serve as a suitable substrate for diverse nanostructures. As a resilient and flexible membrane, it can undergo modifications to enhance and facilitate EHS.
Altering the number of graphene layers in the structure can modify the characteristics and electronic properties of the material influencing its dielectric and mechanical properties.

The EHS capability can be enhanced by introducing porous structures and surface functionalization of graphene. Doping graphene with a heteroatom can modify its physical, optical, electrical, and structural properties due to the numerous catalytically active sites acquired. The decoration of a graphene surface with metal NPs and the introduction of a secondary metal-based component to the structure can further optimize its performance.

Graphene modified with affordable and readily accessible heteroatoms (N, B, S) exhibits noteworthy electrocatalytic performance. Compared with N-doped graphene and GO, N and S-co-doped graphene exhibit superior electrical conductivity and electrochemical efficiency. Doping foreign atoms into graphene induces a semiconducting gap, which transforms the material into an n-type semiconductor. Enhancing the electrical conductivity through heteroatom doping leads to an increase in the EHS capability, paving the way for the creation of cost-effective and metal-free catalytic systems for EHS applications.

Graphene nanocomposites exhibit enhanced electrical conductivity and surface area. Graphene-based functional composites combined with metals, alloys, intermetallic compounds, and their hydrides serve as electrode materials for safe and compact EHS. MH-graphene carbon composites can improve the specific capacity of electrodes by 10–20%.

GQDs with their functional associations, exhibit outstanding physicochemical properties, including robust quantum confinement, edge effects, impressive fluorescence, low toxicity, π-π network-based surface grafting, unique spin properties, high surface area, photostability, and crystalline structure. Their band gap energy can be adjusted by modifying their surface properties and size, and they can be doped with heteroatoms (N, B, S) to enhance their electrical and electrochemical properties and catalytic activities. They can also be linked to ternary transition metal sulfide-based materials through functional groups, resulting in hybrid nanostructures with unique structural properties and synergistic electrochemical characteristics.

The 3D graphene structure GF characterized by well-developed interconnected pores and dangling bonds exhibits high capacity and excellent stability suitable for EHS applications. N-doped GFs demonstrate significantly improved performance compared to that of pure graphene due to the higher electronegativity of N, which resulted in a greater number of electroactive sites on the surface.

The hybrid nanomaterial Co-MGs, which exhibit a high hydrogen storage capacity and cycling stability, emerged as promising EHS materials due to the synergistic effect between the Co metal and the host MG.
Utilizing doped graphene as an NP substrate could increase the electrocatalytic activity of SrCrO4(1000) nanocrystals and increase the EHS ability, demonstrating excellent cycling stability.

The CdSnO3/graphene nanocomposite electrode exhibited a better EHS capacity than the pristine CdSnO3 nanostructures. Calcination temperature, ultrasonic waves and solvent type are the factors influencing the structure optimization. Ultrasound irradiation is effective at preventing particle agglomeration.

In evaluating nanocomposites with metal, metal oxide, and carbon components for EHS applications, each component has a crucial role. For instance, Ni–ZrO2–rGO showed a larger surface area than Co–ZrO2–rGO and significantly impacted the EHS capacity. The metal component influences the electrocatalytic properties, with Ni nanocomposite having a greater discharge capacity than Co nanocomposite due to its conductivity and defects, making it superior for hydrogen storage. Zirconia affects the crystalline structure, while graphene causes variations in porosity.

The application of a three-layer GO/Ni/GO coating to the Ni-foam surface reveals a substantial enhancement in the EHS performance due to the high specific area achieved. Extending the coating time leads to more GO coating on the surface, enhancing the storage rate, whereas increasing the voltage beyond optimum causes GO to cover the cavities in the Ni foam, reducing the specific surface area and consequently decreasing the storage rate.

Bifunctional nanocomposites composed of mixed metal oxides (TiNb2O7) with S/N-doped GQDs are effective in EHS applications. S/N-doped GQDs enhance the electron richness, improving the conductivity. Pyridinic-N-oxide generates electrophilic active sites, facilitating hydrogen absorption. The synergistic effects of TN and GQDs lead to excellent conductivity and structure, with the potential for improved EHS capacity through optimization of morphology and electrolyte concentration.

The type of metal salt (Co/Ni), acid/base (TGA/NaOH) amounts, and pH play significant roles in controlling the size, morphology, and composition of the 3D N-GQD/NCS/CS core-double shell spheres. They show promising electrochemical properties suitable for EHS applications. The EHS capacity is enhanced by decorating the CSs with sand-rose-shaped NCS/CoS/Co9S8 nanostructures. The addition of N-GQDs to the second shell, which covers the NCS/CS structure, enhances the EHS behavior of the electrode. The presence of N-GQDs creates trap sites for hydrogen sorption, leading to improved storage performance. The conductivity of the core-double-shell spheres is also enhanced due to the positive impact of N-GQDs, which leads to an improvement in the discharge efficiency.
The addition of GQDs and GO significantly influences the electrochemical behavior of nanostructured BaMoO4/ZnO due to the Mo6+/Mo5+ redox pair reaction at the active sites during the EHS process.

The combination of S-GQDs/N-GQDs (as conductive components) and CoFe2O4 NPs (electrocatalysts) with HNTs (many active sites) leads to the formation of an effective and eco-friendly active EHS material. The ternary nanocomposites comprising CoFe2O4/S and N-GQD/HNT exhibit enhanced EHS performance due to the combination of conductive additives (S-GQDs/N-GQDs), physisorption, and spillover mechanisms.

CoRGO-coated Co9S8 electrode exhibited superior electrochemical performance compared to the RGO-modified alloy and the original alloy. The addition of Co-SiO2 improved the electrode's cycling stability and discharge rate. The Co particles within the electrode actively engaged in reversible redox reactions, while graphene increased the conductivity. The unique reticular globular morphology and 3D structure of CoRGO improved the reaction kinetics by increasing electrocatalytic activity and specific surface area. The synergistic effect of Co and graphene in the CoRGO composite facilitated hydrogen diffusion, expedited charge transfer within/on the alloy, enhanced corrosion resistance, and improved the EHS performance of the alloy.

The introduction of graphene has a significant impact on the EHS performance of the LaY2Ni9.5Mn0.5Al0.5 alloy, improving the exchange current density and the dischargeability of the alloy electrodes, and the diffusion coefficient of hydrogen.

Incorporating GO, NG, and NSG into the Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy improves its EHS capacity, providing enhanced discharge capacity and stability compared to the pristine alloy. The high surface area and conductivity of graphene contribute to this improvement, while co-doping with N and S further enhances the electrocatalytic performance of graphene. The uniform distribution of NSG on the alloy surface creates additional channels for hydrogen transmission, accelerates charge transfer, and increases hydrogen permeability, improving the EHS capacity of Co0.9Cu0.1Si alloy.
Finally, Table 4 outlines the major conclusions based on the literature survey on recent graphene-based materials for EHS research.

According to the investigated studies, graphene-based materials have shown plenty of advantages for EHS applications. Table 5 presents the main advantages and challenges of graphene-based materials in EHS research.

Further studies should be directed toward the production of stable, homogeneous, monodisperse, and high-quality graphene materials at the industrial scale to solve the limitations in the applications of graphene. There are limited studies on the utilization of GQDs in EHS systems, although highlighting the high efficiency of GQD incorporation in electrode structures. Developing a scalable synthesis method for generating high-quality GQDs remains should be conducted.

The experimentally accessible surface area of graphene can decrease significantly due to the stacking of graphene layers, which arises from the strong tendency of graphene layers to aggregate. To address this challenge, studies should be directed toward the synthesis of novel graphene structures, featuring a porous structure with an interconnected pore network, ensuring appropriate pathways and channels for rapid electron transfer and providing exceptional electrical conductivity, also low density and weight, a high specific surface area and strength-to-weight ratio.

Further studies should be conducted to develop novel graphene-based structures with higher efficiency and cyclic stability and to synthesize novel materials that reduce the distance between ion diffusion and the charge transport path to improve electrode kinetics and mass transport.

The insights and recommendations derived from this study can serve as valuable references and guidance for comprehending the recent applications and future prospects of EHS and graphene within the field of electrochemistry.

Turkan Kopac: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.
